ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for crepe cake or mille crepe; mapped to closest parent class 'cake food product' (FOODON_03301075) as the most defensible ancestor available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for crepe cake; A00KP (Cakes and pastries) is the closest defensible parent category match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for crepe cake; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical slice of crepe cake weighs approximately 120 g, representing one-eighth of a standard 20 cm round crepe cake.

nutrient_source.title: Crepe Cake — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Crepe Cake (F02010009), confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cream-filled layer cake (NDB 18139) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; estimation documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate using layer cake / cream-filled cake GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for crepe cake in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 67 (medium) is estimated from the closest analogous food: cream-filled sponge/layer cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which report GI values of 60–72 for cream-filled cakes. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (67 × 20.88) / 100 ≈ 14.0 for a 120 g slice containing approximately 20.88 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (8.5 g/100 g) with very low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100 g); sodium is low at 57 mg/100 g but added sugar load promotes insulin resistance and fluid retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit crepe cake to very small occasional portions (≤60 g) as the high sugar and saturated fat content can exacerbate insulin resistance and indirectly elevate blood pressure, despite the low sodium level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (67) combined with 8.5 g/100 g sugar, minimal dietary fibre (0.6 g/100 g), and high saturated fat impairs postprandial glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit crepe cake to a single thin slice (≤60 g) on special occasions only, pairing with a high-fibre food to blunt the glycaemic response, and should monitor blood glucose 2 hours post-consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 10.5 g/100 g from heavy cream and butter substantially exceeds the AHA recommended limit of 5–6% of daily energy from saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat crepe cake as an occasional indulgence only, limiting portion size to ≤60 g and avoiding frequent consumption, as the high saturated fat content raises LDL cholesterol and increases atherosclerotic risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g far exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; high sugar with negligible fibre promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of crepe cake as its high saturated fat and sugar content promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways; no practical modification makes this food RA-suitable in its standard form.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crepe cake, also known as mille crêpe or mille-crepe cake, is a French-inspired layered dessert composed of numerous thin pan-fried crepes stacked with cream filling between each layer. The crepes are made from a batter of wheat flour, eggs, milk, butter, and sugar, while the filling typically consists of sweetened whipped cream, pastry cream, or mascarpone. The finished cake is chilled before slicing to allow the layers to set. The texture is delicate and silky, with a subtle eggy flavour from the crepes contrasting with the rich, lightly sweet cream. Nutritionally, crepe cake is energy-dense at 253 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 17.89 g/100 g, of which an estimated 10.5 g is saturated fat derived from heavy cream and butter. Carbohydrates are moderate at 17.4 g/100 g with 8.5 g sugar, and dietary fibre is very low at 0.6 g/100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 67 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 14 per standard 120 g slice. Clinically, this dessert is unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high saturated fat and sugar load. Sodium is low at 57 mg/100 g. Calcium is notable at 94 mg/100 g from the dairy components. Crepe cake is popular in Singapore, Japan, and across Southeast Asia as a café dessert and celebration cake.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of crepe cake?
Is crepe cake high in fat?
Is crepe cake suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of crepe cake?
How much sugar is in crepe cake?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (F02010009) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cream-filled layer cake analogue. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous cream-filled cake entries. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.5 g/100 g) is an estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central entry for cream-filled layer cake (NDB 18139) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This value is used for health context calculations and is flagged as estimated. 2. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed data is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F02010009) or the USDA FoodData Central cream-filled cake entries as of 2026, and no other approved source provides these values for crepe cake in its standard preparation: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not separately reported by HPB or USDA for this composite dessert; potassium_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo for this item and not available in USDA cream-filled cake analogue at the required specificity; cholesterol_mg — not separately reported by HPB for this item; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for crepe cake and not available in USDA FoodData Central cream-filled cake analogue at the required specificity; phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — mineral panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this item; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for crepe cake. Sources consulted and confirmed unavailable: HPB Singapore Foodo (F02010009, accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central NDB 18139 (accessed 2026-04-16). 3. GI of 67 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database cream-filled cake entries; no direct GI measurement for crepe cake exists in the database. 4. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 120 g slice: (67 × 20.88) / 100 ≈ 14.0. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; crepe cake may contain non-halal gelatin or alcohol-based flavourings depending on preparation. 6. This food is a Western / French-inspired dessert with no mandatory Singaporean multilingual naming requirement; local_names fields are null as appropriate.
